title: Contact topology meets thermodynamics
speaker: Leonid Polterovich
affiliation: Tel Aviv University and University of Chicago
abstract: I will discuss the occurrence of some notions and results from contact topology in the context of equilibrium and non-equilibrium thermodynamics. Based on joint works with Michael Entov and Lenya Ryzhik.

proof_stages.label: Contact-topological setup and Reeb interlinking
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames equilibrium thermodynamics as contact geometry, fixes the jet-space contact model with Legendrians and Reeb chords, and states robust interlinking criteria guaranteeing chords under positive contact Hamiltonians.

proof_stages.label: Positive Legendrian order
proof_stages.summary: Nonnegative contact and Legendrian paths are used to define an order relation, with the zero-section class in the jet bundle serving as an orderable class where positive order implies interlinking.

proof_stages.label: Equilibrium thermodynamics as generated Legendrians
proof_stages.summary: Given probability densities, entropy, a parameter-dependent Hamiltonian, and free energy, the Gibbs minimizer and generalized pressures are packaged as a Legendrian submanifold generated by the free-energy functional.

proof_stages.label: Fast relaxation as free-energy gradient flow
proof_stages.summary: Irreversible relaxation is modeled by decreasing free energy via the Otto-metric gradient flow, yielding a Fokker-Planck equation and raising the comparison with stochastic microscopic dynamics.

proof_stages.label: Mean-field spin thermodynamic limit and singular Legendrian
proof_stages.summary: For the Curie-Weiss mean-field spin system, reduction to magnetization gives explicit finite-particle equilibrium Legendrians whose thermodynamic limit develops a nonsmooth phase-transition front with stable, metastable, and unstable branches.

proof_stages.label: Parameter jumps and Reeb chords as ultrafast relaxation
proof_stages.summary: After an instantaneous temperature and magnetic-field jump, the probability distribution is initially unchanged, and at special parameter values the limiting initial and final Gibbs states coincide, making the contact intersection a Reeb chord interpreted as ultrafast relaxation.

proof_stages.label: Slow processes linked to ultrafast chords by order
proof_stages.summary: Quasi-static slow processes are modeled as nonnegative paths of Legendrians, and the order/interlinking theorem implies that a slow passage from one equilibrium system to another forces an ultrafast Reeb-chord process in the corresponding direction.

proof_stages.label: Metastability, dictionary, and open extensions
proof_stages.summary: The closing discussion explains how nonequilibrium dynamics detects metastable branches, summarizes the contact-thermodynamic dictionary, and lists open problems about nonsmooth Legendrian limits, chord stability, microscopic perturbations, and broader models.

examples.name: Glauber/Monte-Carlo stochastic relaxation
examples.note: The audience compares the Otto/Fokker-Planck relaxation flow with Monte Carlo or Glauber-type Markov-chain dynamics, and the speaker later uses this as a familiar stochastic-relaxation model whose thermodynamic limit should be related to the contact picture.
examples.engagement_reason: Glauber/Monte-Carlo relaxation is only contrasted with the speaker’s gradient/Fokker–Planck-type flow and later cited as a Markov-chain model, without running or computing that stochastic dynamics.

examples.name: Very slow heating
examples.note: Slowly increasing the temperature is used as a concrete quasi-static process, modeled contact-geometrically by a nonnegative family of equilibrium Legendrians.
examples.engagement_reason: Very slow heating is given as a typical quasi-static process, but no concrete heating path is analyzed beyond the general statement that such processes are modeled by nonnegative Legendrian families.

examples.name: Supercooled and superheated water
examples.note: Supercooled water freezing when shaken, and superheated microwave water erupting, illustrate metastable states that persist for a long time but rapidly move to a stable equilibrium after perturbation.
examples.engagement_reason: Supercooled water freezing when shaken and overheated microwave water are cited as familiar metastability examples, with no calculation or detailed analysis of them.

passes.reason: metastable water is used as a familiar illustration of long-lived nonequilibrium behavior
supercooled and superheated water are ordinary metastable physical states used to illustrate metastability

examples.name: Second-order transition at zero magnetic field
examples.note: An audience member contrasts the first-order field-flip transition with the zero-field temperature-driven transition where a higher derivative is singular, illustrating a class of phase transitions outside the main setup.
examples.engagement_reason: The zero-field second-order transition is raised in a question and characterized qualitatively by a second-derivative singularity, but the speaker does not analyze it further.
